mission.title: Install and Configure Kcl on Kubernetes
mission.description: KCL is an open-source, constraint-based record and functional programming language designed to enhance the writing of complex configurations, particularly in cloud-native scenarios. Installing KCL allows you to generate and manage configuration data more effectively, reducing boilerplate and improving modularity.

steps.title: Add the KCL Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official KCL Helm repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add kcl-lang https://artifacthub.github.io/helm-charts --namespace kcl --create-namespace
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KCL using Helm
steps.description:
Install KCL in your Kubernetes cluster using the Helm chart.
```bash
helm install kcl kcl-lang/kcl --namespace kcl --create-namespace --version 0.11.2
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the KCL pods are running successfully in the kcl namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kcl
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
Forward a local port to the KCL service to access it from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/kcl --namespace kcl 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KCL will show the KCL pods running in the kcl namespace when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace kcl`. You should also be able to access the KCL service via `http://localhost:8080` after setting up port forwarding.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kcl-lang https://artifacthub.github.io/helm-charts --namespace kcl --create-namespace
helm repo update

kubectl edit deployment kcl --namespace kcl
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     memory: "512Mi"
#     cpu: "500m"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the KCL release from your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. This will remove the deployment and associated resources managed by Helm.
```bash
helm uninstall kcl --namespace kcl
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
If there are any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) or persistent volume claims (PVCs) created by KCL, clean them up to free up resources.
```bash
kubectl delete crd <crd-name>
# Replace <crd-name> with the actual CRD name
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace kcl
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the KCL namespace and verify that all resources have been removed. This step is optional but recommended to clean up the environment.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kcl
kubectl get all --namespace kcl
# Ensure no resources are listed
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's crucial to backup the current state of your KCL installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kcl -o yaml > kcl-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and run the upgrade command to install the latest version of KCL. Ensure you specify the version you want to upgrade to.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kcl kcl-lang/kcl --namespace kcl --version 0.12.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, verify that the KCL pods are running the new version successfully in the kcl namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kcl
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If KCL pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the pod logs for errors that might indicate the cause.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kcl
# Replace <pod-name> with the actual pod name
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the KCL service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly and that the service is running.
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kcl
# Check if the service is listed and running
```

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If the KCL pods are failing to start due to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set in the deployment.
```bash
kubectl describe deployment kcl --namespace kcl
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, verify that you are using the correct namespace and release name.
```bash
helm list --namespace kcl
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and Helm installed on your local machine.
